mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X3 2.0d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X3 2.0d engine typically costs £2,500–£5,000 supply only depending on generation, with the M47 lower and the B47 higher. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500. Main dealer replacements can exceed £6,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your X3.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X3 2.0d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends which generation your X3 is. The M47 (2003–2007) is the most robust. The N47 (2007–2015) has a well‑documented timing chain weakness — many specialists recommend replacing the chain at 100,000 miles. The B47 (2015+) is significantly more reliable with the chain relocated to the front, though EGR cooler failures and DPF clogging remain watchpoints. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X3 2.0d engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: M47: injection pump and turbo issues. N47: timing chain stretch and EGR cooler failure. B47: EGR cooler failure (subject to recall), DPF clogging, and occasional timing chain wear at very high mileage. Each generation has its own character and watchpoints.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an X3 2.0d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. An E83 X3 2.0d is a £2,000–£6,000 vehicle — a £2,500–£3,500 recon is 42–175% of the car's value. For lower‑value examples, replacement may exceed our 70% BMW scrap threshold. An F25 X3 2.0d is a £5,000–£15,000 vehicle — a £3,500–£4,500 recon is 23–90% of the car's value. A G01 X3 2.0d is a £15,000–£30,000 vehicle — a £4,000–£5,000 recon is 13–33% of the car's value, making replacement a clear decision. Evaluate carefully based on your car's generation and condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X3 2.0d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The X3 2.0d covers M47D20 (E83 2003–2007), N47D20A/C (E83 2007–2010 & F25 2010–2015), B47D20A (F25 LCI 2015–2017), and B47D20B (G01 2017+). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm which one you have for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the common failures on an X3 2.0d?

acceptedAnswer.text: On N47 engines: timing chain replacement at 100,000 miles is strongly recommended, regardless of service history. On B47 engines: check for EGR cooler recall completion, regular long runs for DPF health, and BMW‑spec oil changes with shortened intervals (5,000–8,000 miles). At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, coolant loss or DPF warning, book an inspection immediately.

eraMap.title: The X3 2.0d THROUGH TIME — Era Map
eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the range across all generations; specific costs vary by era — see the Era Map above. A reconditioned N47 or B47 engine typically falls between £1,200 and £2,800 for the engine alone, with main dealer replacements quoted as high as £6,800.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an X3 2.0d engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

cta.label: → Compare X3 2.0d engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch (N47, E83 2007–2010 & F25 2010–2015)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking, engine warning light, loss of power, chain rattle on acceleration.
problems.repairCost: £1,200–£2,500 (chain, guides and tensioner replacement) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before chain snaps)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The N47's rear‑mounted timing chain is the defining weakness of this engine — a well‑documented issue that can lead to catastrophic engine failure. If you hear a rattling noise on cold start, have the chain inspected immediately. Many specialists recommend replacing the chain preventatively at 100,000 miles, even with full service history. Regular oil changes with BMW LL‑04 spec oil are essential.

problems.title: EGR Cooler Failure (All Generations — Subject to Recall)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, white exhaust smoke, rough idling, burning smell, engine warning light, engine stalling.
problems.repairCost: £800–£1,500 (EGR cooler replacement, often under recall) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before overheating)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice coolant loss or burning smell, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The EGR cooler is a significant weakness across all BMW diesel engines of this era — BMW issued a recall affecting 800,000 vehicles. The defect can allow glycol coolant to leak, which combined with soot and high temperatures can create a fire risk. If you notice coolant loss, white smoke, or a burning smell, have the EGR cooler inspected immediately. Even after recall work, failures have been reported. Checking recall completion status is essential when buying an X3 2.0d.

problems.title: DPF Clogging (All Generations — Short Journey Use)
problems.symptoms: DPF warning light, reduced engine performance, increased fuel consumption, engine entering limp mode.
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (forced regeneration or DPF clean); £1,500–£3,000 (DPF replacement) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless DPF is physically damaged)
problems.recommendation: Like all modern diesels, the X3 2.0d needs regular long runs for passive DPF regeneration. If your X3 is used primarily for short journeys, plan a 30–45 minute motorway run every two weeks. If the DPF warning light comes on, book a forced regeneration promptly — ignoring it can lead to costly DPF replacement.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This X3 2.0d? Check These First
buyIf: * N47 (E83 2007–2010, F25 2010–2015): timing chain replaced or inspected (or budget for it at 100k)
* B47 (F25 LCI 2015+, G01): EGR cooler recall work completed (check with BMW dealer)
* Documented DPF regeneration history or regular long‑distance driving
* Full BMW service history with 5,000–8,000‑mile oil changes (not the 18,000‑mile BMW schedule)
avoidIf: * Cold‑start rattle with no timing chain history on N47 — walk away
* Coolant loss with no diagnosis — EGR cooler may be failing
* DPF warning light with no regeneration history — may indicate heavy clogging
* Poor service history — X3 2.0d engines are sensitive to neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The X3 2.0d is a cornerstone of BMW's SUV range — and its engine story is one of continuous improvement. The M47 is the most robust; the N47 is the most problematic (timing chain); the B47 is the most refined and reliable, though EGR cooler and DPF issues remain. Buy on generation‑specific history — if you're looking at an N47, budget for timing chain replacement; if you're looking at a B47, check EGR cooler recall completion. A well‑maintained X3 2.0d is a rewarding, long‑lived family SUV; a neglected one is a £4,000+ bill waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare X3 2.0d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X3 2.0d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or ticking; timing chain indicator (N47)
* Coolant Level — Check for unexplained loss; EGR cooler failure indicator
* EGR Recall — Confirm EGR cooler recall work completed (check with BMW dealer)
* Service History — Check oil change intervals (5,000–8,000 miles ideal; 18,000 miles is a red flag)
* DPF Status — Check for warning lights; short journeys can cause clogging
* Turbo Health — Listen for whistling or whining; turbo failure indicator
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
